summary: The paper makes a genuine empirical contribution -- the first cross-domain absorption characterization -- but suffers from six critical/major issues: (1) severe data provenance confusion between consolidation summary, full-mode data files, and the paper text, producing conflicting numbers for the same quantities; (2) the '100% pathological' claim is tested on only one hierarchy (city-continent) and generalized without adequate caveat; (3) the probe quality confound is acknowledged but insufficiently addressed -- RAVEL absorption rates are upper bounds with unknown bias magnitude; (4) the N values reported in the Kruskal-Wallis test (3,545) appear to exclude first-letter, but the paper implies a 4-hierarchy comparison while the actual test covers only 3 RAVEL hierarchies; (5) three missing figure PDFs block compilation of the paper's most important visual evidence; (6) the benign/pathological diagnostic design has a fundamental methodological issue -- it ablates the parent direction from the child decoder vector, not from the model's computation, so the large logit changes may measure decoder geometry rather than causal computational dependence.

findings.description: Data provenance chaos: the consolidation_summary.json reports pilot/iter_008 numbers (first-letter L24_16k = 0.345, city-continent L24 F1 = 0.84, benign instances = 1,464), the full-mode data files report different numbers (first-letter L24_16k = 0.2707, city-continent L24 F1 = 0.871, benign instances counted over 1,471), and the paper uses a third set of numbers in some places (weighted F1 = 0.97 for first-letter L24 vs. 1.0 in the data file). The paper text states N=3,545 for the Kruskal-Wallis test (1,330+1,073+1,142), which exactly matches the full-mode cross-domain file. But the consolidation summary reports ANOVA p=0.005 (pilot-era value), while the paper reports Kruskal-Wallis p=7.4e-66 (full-mode). No single document serves as the authoritative data source, and the paper never states which run's data it uses.
findings.suggestion: Create a single authoritative data manifest that maps every number in the paper to exactly one data file, line number, and field. Resolve all discrepancies: (a) first-letter weighted F1 at L24 is 1.0 in the full-mode file (per_letter_f1 all 1.0 except x=null with 0 samples) -- the paper should report 1.0 or explain why 0.97 is used; (b) benign/pathological instance count should be 1,464 (consolidation) or 1,471 (paper) -- pick one and verify; (c) document that absorption_rate=0.2707 (per-unique-word) vs. 0.3448 (per-instance) for first-letter L24_16k, and explain the aggregation in Section 3.3.

findings.description: The benign/pathological diagnostic (Section 5.3) has a fundamental methodological ambiguity. The procedure ablates the parent direction from the child feature's decoder vector, then measures the logit change. But this measures whether the child feature's decoder carries parent-relevant information in its geometry, NOT whether the model's computation independently uses the parent feature when the child is active. These are different questions. If the child's decoder vector has a large component along the parent direction (which is precisely what absorption IS), then ablating that component will always produce large logit changes. The diagnostic is partly circular: it measures the magnitude of the absorbed component, not the causal independence of the parent's computational role. A proper benign/pathological test would ablate the parent feature's activation directly and measure downstream effects, holding the child constant.
findings.suggestion: Reframe the benign/pathological result more carefully. The current test shows that 'the child feature decoder carries significant parent-direction information' -- not that 'the model needs the parent feature independently.' A proper test of computational redundancy would (a) ablate the parent feature activation (not direction from child decoder) and measure logit change, or (b) perform path patching to determine if the parent direction has independent causal effects through separate downstream circuits. Acknowledge this distinction in the paper. The 3.98 nats result establishes that absorption has large magnitude, not that it is necessarily pathological in the computational-redundancy sense.

findings.description: The Kruskal-Wallis test with p=7.4e-66 and N=3,545 is computed only over the 3 RAVEL hierarchies, explicitly excluding first-letter. The paper's framing ('Absorption rates vary 4x across hierarchies') implies a 4-hierarchy comparison, but the statistical test backing this claim excludes the first-letter baseline. The permutation tests comparing each RAVEL hierarchy against first-letter are reported separately with Bonferroni correction, but only city-language reaches significance (p_Bonf=0.003 at 16k, p_Bonf=0.043 at 65k). City-continent and city-country do NOT differ significantly from first-letter after Bonferroni correction (p_Bonf=1.0 for both at 16k). This means the statistically supported claim is actually: 'city-language absorbs significantly less than first-letter; city-continent and city-country do not differ significantly from first-letter.' The '4x range' is a descriptive observation, not a statistically validated cross-domain effect for most hierarchy pairs.
findings.suggestion: Run the Kruskal-Wallis test including all 4 hierarchies (requires matching the first-letter data to the same per-entity format). Alternatively, clearly separate the descriptive claim ('rates range from 11.6% to 45.1%') from the inferential claim ('only city-language differs significantly from first-letter after Bonferroni correction'). The current framing implies that all four hierarchies differ from each other, which the pairwise tests do not support.

findings.description: The '100% pathological' claim (H8 falsified) is tested exclusively on city-continent at L24. The paper extrapolates to all absorption ('Absorption is 100% pathological' in Section 1, 'absorption is always pathological' in Section 5.3, 'Every absorption instance degrades model output' in Section 5.3). This extrapolation from one hierarchy (city-continent, F1=0.87) to all hierarchies is not warranted. The paper acknowledges this in one sentence ('extending the diagnostic to first-letter and other hierarchies would strengthen the universality claim') but the headline framing treats it as universal.
findings.suggestion: Change the framing from 'absorption is always pathological' to 'absorption is pathological in city-continent at L24' or 'absorption is pathological across all 1,471 tested instances from city-continent.' Add a caveat that the benign/pathological diagnostic has not been run on first-letter, city-language, or city-country, and that first-letter's concentrated absorption mechanism (Section 5.1) might yield a different benign/pathological ratio. The universality claim requires testing on at least the first-letter hierarchy where probe quality is perfect.

findings.description: Probe quality confound is not adequately controlled. RAVEL probes achieve F1=0.73-0.87 at L24. The paper states that measured absorption rates are 'upper bounds' because probe errors inflate false negative counts, but never quantifies the bias. A simple back-of-envelope: if the city-country probe has F1=0.73, roughly 27% of instances are misclassified on raw activations. The 'probe-correct' instances used as denominator already exclude some of these, but the remaining false-positive probe-correct instances (cases where the raw probe is correct by chance) contaminate the denominator. More importantly, the paper never reports the probe-only FN rate (control 3 from Section 3.3): what fraction of probe-correct instances on raw activations become probe-incorrect on raw activations with a different random sample? Without this control, we cannot bound the probe-induced inflation.
findings.suggestion: Report the probe-only baseline (control 3) numerically for each hierarchy. Compute the expected false negative rate attributable to probe imperfection alone (e.g., by applying the probe to a held-out set of raw activations and measuring inconsistency). If this baseline is, say, 5%, then a measured absorption rate of 11.6% (city-language) has a meaningful signal-to-noise ratio, but a rate of 45.1% (city-country with F1=0.73) could have substantial probe-induced inflation. The shuffled hierarchy control is not the same thing -- it controls for SAE reconstruction error, not for probe boundary effects.

findings.description: The cross-domain activation patching (Section 5.2) uses 93 entities with 50 contexts each yielding 3,751 FN instances, but the child feature identification for RAVEL hierarchies may be fundamentally different from first-letter. For first-letter, a 'child feature' is well-defined (the word-specific feature). For city-continent, what constitutes a 'child feature' for the parent concept 'Europe'? The paper identifies it via integrated-gradients attribution (the active feature with highest attribution to the probe's changed prediction), but this is a correlational identification. If the absorption is distributed, no single feature will have high attribution, and zeroing the 'identified child' may simply be zeroing a random feature. The 0.05% recovery rate and 14.5% control recovery suggest the identified child features are noise. The paper reframes this as 'distributed vs. concentrated absorption,' but an equally valid interpretation is 'the child feature identification method fails for multi-class hierarchies.'
findings.suggestion: Discuss the alternative interpretation: integrated-gradients child identification may fail when absorption is distributed across many features, producing spurious 'child' identifications. Test whether the identified child features have any special relationship to the hierarchy by checking: (a) does the identified child's decoder have above-chance cosine similarity with the parent probe direction? (b) does zeroing the top-5 or top-10 attributed features improve recovery? If multi-feature zeroing shows higher recovery, the 'distributed absorption' interpretation strengthens. If not, the child identification method may simply be broken for RAVEL.

findings.description: Three figure PDFs are missing (fig4_patching_comparison.pdf, fig5_pathological_histogram.pdf, fig6_architecture_comparison.pdf), corresponding to three of the paper's five main contributions. A reviewer cannot evaluate the visual evidence for the causal mechanism, the pathological absorption distribution, or the architecture comparison. Table 3 is inline markdown while all other tables are PDF figures, creating a format inconsistency that will break LaTeX compilation.
findings.suggestion: Generate all missing figures from the data files before any review or compilation. Convert Table 3 to the same format as the other tables. This is a blocking issue for the paper.

findings.description: The paper's first-letter absorption rates differ substantially between the full-mode data file (L24_16k: 0.2707 per-unique-word, 0.3448 per-instance) and the consolidation summary (0.345). The paper uses 0.2707 (27.1%), but the consolidation uses 0.345. This discrepancy is never explained. The full-mode data file also shows that first-letter probe raw accuracy at L24 is only 0.759 (75.9% of instances have correct raw predictions), which is surprisingly low for probes with F1=1.0. This suggests the first-letter absorption pipeline uses 3 prompt templates per word (n_prompts_per_word=3) and many words have failing prompts, meaning the 'F1=1.0' is measured on a different set of instances than the absorption rate. The per-instance vs. per-unique-word distinction is methodologically important and undocumented.
findings.suggestion: Add a paragraph to Section 3.3 explaining: (a) absorption rates are computed per unique word/entity (averaging across prompt contexts); (b) why this aggregation is chosen (prevents dominant entities from inflating rates); (c) the raw probe accuracy is 75.9% at L24 even though F1=1.0, because different prompt templates elicit different information. Also clarify that the F1=1.0 probes are evaluated on a separate held-out set (1,033 test words) using the sae-spelling pipeline, while absorption rates are computed on 500 test words x 3 prompts = 1,500 instances.

findings.description: The paper claims 'first cross-domain absorption characterization,' but the novelty may be partially undermined by the probe quality limitation. The cross-domain variation (4x range) could be partly or entirely driven by probe quality differences: F1=1.0 for first-letter vs. F1=0.73-0.87 for RAVEL. Worse probes produce more false negatives (the numerator of absorption rate), inflating absorption. The paper acknowledges this ('upper bounds') but does not quantify the possible inflation. If, after correcting for probe quality, the cross-domain variation shrinks to 2x or less, the headline finding weakens substantially.
findings.suggestion: Perform a sensitivity analysis: estimate the probe-quality-adjusted absorption rate by computing the expected number of probe-induced false negatives (from the probe's test set error rate) and subtracting them from the measured FN count. Even a crude adjustment would bound the confound. Report the adjusted rates alongside the raw rates. If city-language (F1=0.82, rate=11.6%) still differs significantly from first-letter (F1=1.0, rate=27.1%) after adjustment, the cross-domain finding is robust. The current paper cannot distinguish 'real cross-domain variation' from 'probe quality artifact' for city-country (F1=0.73, rate=45.1%).

findings.description: The architecture comparison (Section 6) is underpowered. At L24, only 3 SAE configurations from 2 architecture families are available (JumpReLU 16k, JumpReLU 65k, Matryoshka 32k). At L12, where all 4 architectures are available, RAVEL probes are below quality gate. The Kruskal-Wallis test on architecture has only 12 data points at L24 (3 architectures x 4 hierarchies), making it unlikely to detect moderate effects. The width mismatch (Matryoshka 32k vs. JumpReLU 16k/65k) is documented but not controlled.
findings.suggestion: Acknowledge more prominently that the architecture null result is an absence-of-evidence finding with low statistical power, not evidence of absence. The conclusion 'hierarchy dominates architecture' is not warranted by p=0.50 from a 12-observation Kruskal-Wallis test. The correct interpretation is 'we could not detect an architecture effect with the available data.'

findings.description: The paper uses probes trained at token position -6 for first-letter and -2 for RAVEL, without explaining why different positions are used. The first-letter pipeline follows sae-spelling convention (position -6 encodes first character of next word), while RAVEL uses -2 ('the final position before the entity token that most reliably encodes attribute information'). This asymmetry in probe training protocol is a confound: the two positions may differ in how much SAE encoding disrupts the probed information, independent of absorption.
findings.suggestion: Test whether position choice affects absorption rates by training first-letter probes at position -2 (or RAVEL probes at -6) and comparing. If infeasible, acknowledge the position asymmetry as a confound in Section 7.3.

findings.description: The paper reports the Kruskal-Wallis test statistic as H=299.95 with p=7.4e-66. With N=3,545, this p-value is plausible but extraordinarily small. No multiple-testing correction is applied across the multiple Kruskal-Wallis tests (hierarchy at L12, hierarchy at L24, architecture at L12, architecture at L24). While the hierarchy p-values are small enough to survive any correction, the paper does not mention this consideration.
findings.suggestion: Add a sentence noting that Bonferroni correction across the 4 Kruskal-Wallis tests does not affect any conclusion (the smallest p is 7.4e-66, the largest non-significant p is 0.75). This pre-empts a reviewer comment.

metric_gaming_risks: Probe quality inflation: RAVEL probes with F1=0.73-0.87 produce false negatives that are counted as absorption. The measured absorption rates are upper bounds with unknown bias magnitude. City-country (F1=0.73, rate=45.1%) is most vulnerable.

Aggregation method gaming: the paper reports per-unique-word absorption rates (0.2707 for first-letter L24_16k) rather than per-instance rates (0.3448), which systematically lowers the reported rates. While per-unique-word is defensible, the choice is undocumented and the alternative rate is 28% higher.
The 'distributed absorption' interpretation for the failed cross-domain patching could be an artifact of broken child-feature identification rather than a genuine mechanistic distinction.

The 100% pathological finding may be partly tautological: ablating the parent direction from a decoder vector that explicitly carries parent information will always produce large logit changes. This measures absorption magnitude, not computational redundancy.
novelty_risks: The cross-domain novelty claim depends on probe quality being adequate. If probe-quality-adjusted absorption rates show less variation, the cross-domain finding weakens.

Chanin et al. (2024) tested absorption on multiple models (GPT-2 Small, Gemma, Llama, Qwen) but always on first-letter. This paper tests multiple hierarchies but only on one model (Gemma 2 2B). The cross-model vs. cross-hierarchy novelty axes are complementary, and this paper's scope is clearly stated, but generalization to other models is untested.

The 'concentrated vs. distributed absorption' mechanistic claim is intriguing but currently rests on a single contrast (first-letter d=1.33 vs. city-continent d=-0.91). With only 2 data points, this is an observation, not a validated mechanistic theory. Additional hierarchies are needed to test whether the divide is systematic.
reproducibility_gaps: Token position asymmetry: first-letter uses position -6, RAVEL uses position -2. No justification for why different positions are used, and no control for position effects on absorption.

The probe training uses different strategies: sae-spelling pipeline for first-letter (binary one-vs-all) vs. scikit-learn multi-class logistic regression for RAVEL. These are different probe architectures measuring different things.
Code is promised for public release but not yet available.
The full-mode data file and consolidation summary report different numbers for many quantities. A future replicator would need to know which run's data the paper uses.
The specific SAE checkpoints (Gemma Scope canonical) are version-identified but the exact SAEBench checkpoint versions (BatchTopK, Matryoshka) are not specified beyond 'SAEBench.'
